original_hypothesis.title: withdrawRemainingTitn Permanent DoS via Arithmetic Underflow
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In withdrawRemainingTitn(), the calculation `unclaimedTitn = remainingTitnAfter1Year - initialTotalClaimable` will underflow (revert in Solidity 0.8+) if the contract's TITN balance at snapshot time is less than totalTitnClaimable. This occurs when TGT deposits exceed TGT_TO_EXCHANGE (ECON-H001), causing totalTitnClaimable > actual TITN balance. The snapshot is attempted on every call when remainingTitnAfter1Year == 0 (which it stays at if balance < totalTitnClaimable), permanently DoS-ing all post-360-day claims.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a cascading consequence of ECON-H001 that permanently locks all remaining user funds after 360 days. Since claimTitn is blocked after 360 days (>= check), and withdrawRemainingTitn always reverts, users have NO claim path. This creates a dead-end state machine with no recovery.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Line ~117: `uint256 unclaimedTitn = remainingTitnAfter1Year - initialTotalClaimable` — no check that remainingTitnAfter1Year >= initialTotalClaimable. If contract balance < totalTitnClaimable at snapshot, underflow reverts permanently.

preconditions: Over-allocation occurred (ECON-H001) OR owner withdrew TITN via withdraw()
At least one user has claimableTitnPerUser > 0

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: unclaimedTitn = remainingTitnAfter1Year - initialTotalClaimable with no underflow guard
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Over-deposit of TGT makes contract balance < totalTitnClaimable at 360-day mark
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Not a profit attack but permanent fund lock: all unclaimed TITN becomes irrecoverable

economic_questions: Under what over-allocation thresholds does the underflow occur?
If some users claimed before 360d, does partial claiming prevent the underflow?
Is there any recovery path once the underflow condition is set?
validation_requirements: Simulate: deposit > 579M TGT, advance to 360d, call withdrawRemainingTitn and verify revert
Verify re-initialization loop: remainingTitnAfter1Year stays 0, every call re-enters init and underflows
Confirm no admin escape hatch (no function to manually set snapshot values)

preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Permanent once 360-day mark is reached with insolvent state

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Condition is latent; manifests automatically at 360-day mark
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: All unclaimed TITN permanently locked; users who delayed claiming lose everything

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All users who did not claim via claimTitn before 360 days
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 100% loss of claimable TITN plus proportional bonus
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: totalTitnClaimable at 360-day mark (could be majority of TITN_ARB)

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Track totalTitnClaimable vs titn.balanceOf(contract) over time
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm: if balanceOf < totalTitnClaimable, subtraction reverts; and remainingTitnAfter1Year stays 0 causing perpetual re-initialization

validation_result.impact_summary: If the contract's TITN balance is less than the total claimable TITN at the 360-day mark, `withdrawRemainingTitn` will consistently revert, permanently locking all user claims.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The function `withdrawRemainingTitn()` is designed to distribute remaining TITN proportionally among users who wait until after 360 days to claim. On the first call, it snapshots the contract's TITN balance (`remainingTitnAfter1Year`) and the total liabilities (`initialTotalClaimable`). It then calculates the surplus as `unclaimedTitn = remainingTitnAfter1Year - initialTotalClaimable`. However, if the contract is undercollateralized (e.g., due to over-allocation of TGT deposits or the owner withdrawing TITN), `remainingTitnAfter1Year` will be less than `initialTotalClaimable`. Because Solidity 0.8+ has built-in overflow/underflow checks, this subtraction will revert. Since the revert undoes the state changes in the initialization block, `remainingTitnAfter1Year` remains 0, causing every subsequent call to attempt the same initialization and fail. This creates a permanent Denial of Service (DoS) for all post-360-day claims.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code explicitly performs `remainingTitnAfter1Year - initialTotalClaimable` without checking if `remainingTitnAfter1Year >= initialTotalClaimable`. The initialization block is guarded by `if (remainingTitnAfter1Year == 0)`, meaning a revert during this calculation will leave the state uninitialized, leading to a perpetual failure loop.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The subtraction `remainingTitnAfter1Year - initialTotalClaimable` assumes the contract's TITN balance is always greater than or equal to the total claimable TITN. If the balance is lower, this subtraction underflows and reverts. The revert prevents `remainingTitnAfter1Year` from being permanently set, causing all future calls to re-enter the initialization block and revert again.

calling_sequence: unclaimedTitn = remainingTitnAfter1Year - initialTotalClaimable underflows and reverts
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: When `withdrawRemainingTitn` is called, it checks `if (remainingTitnAfter1Year == 0)` and sets `remainingTitnAfter1Year = currentRemainingTitn` and `initialTotalClaimable = totalTitnClaimable`. It then executes `uint256 unclaimedTitn = remainingTitnAfter1Year - initialTotalClaimable;`. If `currentRemainingTitn < totalTitnClaimable`, the subtraction underflows, reverting the transaction. The state changes are rolled back, leaving `remainingTitnAfter1Year` at 0, which guarantees the next caller will face the exact same underflow.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario is highly realistic and requires no direct exploitation by an attacker, as it can manifest naturally from aggregate user behavior (over-depositing TGT) or admin actions (withdrawing TITN). Once the 360-day mark is reached with an undercollateralized state, the contract enters a dead-end state machine where no user can claim their funds.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL. All unclaimed TITN becomes permanently locked in the contract. Users who waited until after 360 days to claim their tokens (which is incentivized by the proportional bonus mechanism) will suffer a 100% loss of their funds.
recommended_mitigations: Implement a cap on TGT deposits to ensure `totalTitnClaimable` never exceeds the contract's TITN balance.
Use a conditional check to handle undercollateralization gracefully: `uint256 unclaimedTitn = remainingTitnAfter1Year > initialTotalClaimable ? remainingTitnAfter1Year - initialTotalClaimable : 0;`.
Restrict the `withdraw` function so the owner cannot withdraw TITN that is allocated to users.
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy MergeTgt and Titn contracts. 2. Deposit TITN into MergeTgt. 3. Call `onTokenTransfer` with enough TGT to make `totalTitnClaimable > titn.balanceOf(MergeTgt)`. 4. Advance time by 360 days. 5. Attempt to call `withdrawRemainingTitn` from a user with claimable TITN and observe the Arithmetic Underflow revert.
